pmos

Experimentation & Statistics — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Growth. Experimentation is how product teams convert opinion into evidence, and statistics is the grammar that keeps the evidence honest. An online experiment is just a randomized controlled experiment on a product surface; randomization is the whole trick, and the leaders who run tens of thousands a year — Kohavi, Tang & Xu's Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments is the canonical text — insist the hard part is trust, not math. The inferential machinery rests on the null hypothesis and a chosen significance level, summarized by a p-value that almost everyone misreads — which is exactly why the American Statistical Association issued a formal warning about it. The more decision-useful outputs are the confidence interval and the effect size, because statistical significance is not practical significance. Designing a test means budgeting power and sample size up front, which is what makes the peeking problem so dangerous and sequential testing so useful. The field's live fault line is frequentist vs. Bayesian — and the popular claim that Bayes makes peeking free does not hold. Practitioners speed tests up with variance reduction (CUPED), defend trust with Twyman's law, and lose results to Simpson's paradox and the multiple comparisons problem. Where the field genuinely disagrees — which paradigm to adopt, whether to always experiment — this primer flags it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 17 verified sources.

What a controlled experiment actually buys you

Strip away the tooling and an online experiment is the oldest idea in empirical science wearing a product UI. An A/B test is, in the precise sense, "a shorthand for a simple randomized controlled experiment, in which a number of samples (e.g. A and B) of a single vector-variable are compared." You split incoming users at random, show one group the change and the other the status quo, and compare a metric. The reason this earns the word causal — and analytics dashboards, cohort comparisons, and before/after rollouts do not — is randomization. Random assignment makes the two groups statistically exchangeable on everything you measured and everything you didn't: device mix, tenure, intent, the weather. Any difference that survives is attributable to the one thing you varied. That is the entire epistemic payload, and it is why teams pay the latency and complexity tax to run experiments instead of just shipping and watching the line.

The contrast worth holding in your head is with observational data, which is most of what a PM swims in. If power users adopt a new feature and also retain better, the dashboard cannot tell you whether the feature drove retention or whether the kind of person who adopts early was always going to retain. The experiment severs that knot by construction. This is also why the discipline scales the way it does: the organizations that treat experimentation as infrastructure rather than a special occasion run staggering volumes. The teams behind Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments — at Microsoft, Google, LinkedIn, and (for Kohavi) Amazon and Airbnb — each run on the order of tens of thousands of controlled experiments a year, which only works when the cost of running one approaches zero and the trust in each result is high enough to act on without a committee.

The strategic point for a senior PM is that experimentation is not primarily a measurement tool; it is a decision-rights tool. It moves the basis of a "ship it" decision from the most senior or most confident voice in the room to a pre-agreed metric and a pre-agreed bar. That cultural shift — institutionalizing the humility that most ideas don't work — is the actual product of an experimentation program, and the statistics are the load-bearing wall that keeps the humility from collapsing into noise-chasing.

The hypothesis and the metric you're allowed to move

Most weak experiments fail before a single user is bucketed, because the team never agreed on what would count as success. A usable hypothesis is falsifiable and quantitative: not "the new onboarding is better" but "the new onboarding raises day-7 activation by at least two percentage points without lifting week-1 churn." That sentence smuggles in three commitments — a direction, a minimum effect worth caring about, and a constraint — and writing it before launch is the single highest-leverage discipline in the whole pipeline.

The canon's name for the success metric is the OEC, the Overall Evaluation Criterion. In the framing of Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments, the OEC "is a quantitative measure of the experiment's objective" — the one metric (or weighted composite) the experiment is licensed to move and be judged on. The art of a good OEC is choosing something that is sensitive enough to move in a two-week window yet causally upstream of the long-term value you actually care about. Clicks are sensitive but easily gamed by a louder button; lifetime value is what you want but moves too slowly to test against. The craft is finding the leading indicator that predicts the lagging one, and the discipline is fixing it before you look at any data.

Equally important and more often skipped: the OEC travels with guardrail metrics — the things that must not degrade. A variant that lifts conversion while quietly raising page-load time, support tickets, or unsubscribes is not a win, and if you didn't pre-declare those guardrails you will rationalize the damage after the fact. Naming one primary metric, a small set of guardrails, and a minimum detectable effect before launch is what separates an experiment from a fishing expedition — and, as the section on multiple comparisons will show, it is also your statistical insurance against finding a "winner" purely because you measured forty things.

The logic of significance testing

The inferential frame underneath classical A/B testing is, at first encounter, backwards — and staying clear-eyed about its shape is what immunizes you against the misreadings that follow. You do not test whether your variant works. You assume it does nothing and ask how surprised you should be by the data. That "does nothing" assumption is the null hypothesis: "the claim in scientific research that the effect being studied does not exist." The test then evaluates the data against that assumption: "if the data-set of a randomly selected representative sample is very unlikely relative to the null hypothesis ... the experimenter rejects the null hypothesis, concluding it (probably) is false." Notice what the machinery can and cannot deliver: rejecting the null is the only affirmative move available. The test never proves your variant works; it only licenses the statement that the no-effect world is an implausible explanation for what you saw.

The threshold for "implausible" is the significance level, α. A result has statistical significance "when a result at least as 'extreme' would be very infrequent if the null hypothesis were true," and α is "the probability of the study rejecting the null hypothesis, given that the null hypothesis is true." Set α to the conventional 0.05 and you are explicitly accepting a 5% false-positive rate: in a world where your change truly does nothing, one test in twenty will still cross the line by chance alone. α is not a discovered truth about your product; it is a budget you set for how often you're willing to be fooled.

This is where two error types enter, and they trade off against each other. A Type I error is a false positive — calling a dud a winner, controlled by α. A Type II error is a false negative — missing a real effect, controlled by power (the next-but-one section). The 2×2 below is worth internalizing because almost every experimentation mistake is a failure to respect one of its cells.

The whole frequentist enterprise is an attempt to hold the top-left cell to a known small rate while making the top-right cell as large as your budget allows. Everything that follows — p-values, intervals, power, the peeking problem — is bookkeeping on this 2×2.

What a p-value is — and the thing it is not

The p-value is the most reported and least understood number in product analytics, and the gap is not a minor academic quibble — it changes what decisions you think the data supports. Formally, a p-value is "the probability of obtaining test results at least as extreme as the result actually observed, under the assumption that the null hypothesis is correct." Read that twice, because the load-bearing phrase is "under the assumption that the null hypothesis is correct." The p-value lives entirely inside the hypothetical world where your change does nothing. It answers: if the variant were inert, how often would noise alone produce a result this striking?

The near-universal error is to flip the conditional. People hear "p = 0.03" and think "there's a 97% chance my variant is better" or "a 3% chance this is a fluke." Both are wrong, and the field's own governing body says so plainly. The p-value does not measure "the probability that the studied hypothesis is true, or the probability that the data were produced by random chance alone." In symbols: the p-value is P(data this extreme | no effect), not P(no effect | data). Those two conditional probabilities can differ by an order of magnitude depending on how plausible your hypothesis was to begin with — which is precisely the gap Bayesian methods try to close, and a major reason that debate exists at all.

The misuse is so entrenched that the American Statistical Association issued a formal 2016 statement — a rare event, a professional body warning against its own field's house metric. Its principles are worth keeping near the dashboard: "P-values do not measure the probability that the studied hypothesis is true, or the probability that the data were produced by random chance alone." And, aimed squarely at how teams actually use the number: "Scientific conclusions and business or policy decisions should not be based only on whether a p-value passes a specific threshold." Finally, the one that kills the most value in product work: "a p-value, or statistical significance, does not measure the size of an effect or the importance of a result." A p-value can be tiny because the effect is large, or tiny because the sample is enormous and the effect is trivial. It cannot, by itself, tell those two worlds apart — which is why the next section argues you should largely stop leading with it.

Confidence intervals and the effect that actually matters

If the p-value answers a binary question badly, the confidence interval answers the question you actually have. A confidence interval is "a range of values which is likely to contain (in repeated sampling) the true value of an unknown statistical parameter." Instead of a yes/no on significance, it hands you the plausible range of the effect — and that range is what a ship/no-ship decision should turn on. A 95% CI on conversion lift of [+0.2%, +0.4%] and one of [+0.2%, +9%] can carry the same p-value while implying completely different decisions; the first is a confirmed small win, the second is a coin flip between trivial and transformative that probably needs more data.

The interpretation of the CI is as slippery as the p-value's and fails the same way — by attaching the probability to the wrong object. The correct frequentist reading is about the procedure across hypothetical repetitions, not about the single interval in front of you: "if the same sampling procedure were repeated 100 times from the same population, approximately 95 of the resulting intervals would be expected to contain the true population mean." Your one interval either contains the truth or it doesn't; the 95% describes the long-run reliability of the method that produced it. (This subtlety, incidentally, is one of Bayesian credible intervals' main selling points: they can be read as "95% probability the effect is in this range," at the cost of requiring a prior.)

Underneath both tools sits the distinction senior PMs must enforce relentlessly: statistical significance is not practical significance. As one widely used practitioner guide puts it, "statistical significance is a tool which allows action to be taken despite random uncertainty," and its dominant failure mode in product work is "confusing high statistical significance with substantive or practically relevant improvement ... a statistically significant result can be for an outcome so small in magnitude that it has no practical value." Wikipedia's own treatment makes the same separation: "the term statistical significance is not the same as research significance, theoretical significance, or practical significance." With enough traffic, every difference becomes statistically significant; the only question that survives at scale is whether the effect is large enough to justify the change and its maintenance cost. Lead with the interval and the effect size; treat the p-value as a footnote.

Power, sample size, and the runtime you're committing to

Power is the half of the 2×2 that teams routinely ignore until a flat result confuses them. Statistical power is "the probability of detecting an effect (i.e. rejecting the null hypothesis) given that some prespecified effect actually exists" — your defense against the Type II error of shipping nothing because a real win hid in the noise. An underpowered test is worse than no test: it spends real traffic and engineering time to produce a "no significant difference" that means nothing, because the experiment never had a realistic chance of detecting the effect you cared about.

Power is not a free parameter; it is the output of three things you can move. Per the canonical treatment, "power increases with the values of the effect size" and "increases with ... the sample size," while it "reduces with increasing variability." Read operationally, that sentence is your test-design checklist. Bigger true effects are easier to catch (which is why you declare a minimum detectable effect — the smallest lift worth detecting — rather than hoping). More users buy more power (which is why low-traffic surfaces are hard to test). And lower variance buys power for free, which is the entire premise of variance reduction later in this primer.

The practical consequence is a commitment you make before launch, not after. A power calculation takes your baseline rate, your chosen MDE, your α, and a power target — 80% is the near-universal convention, meaning you accept a 20% false-negative rate — and returns the sample size you need per arm. That sample size, divided by your traffic, is the runtime. This is the discipline the frequentist paradigm demands and the one teams most often skip: it "requires a fixed sample size, set in advance. Underpowered tests miss effects; overpowered tests detect trivial ones." Fixing that number in advance is not bureaucracy — it is the precondition that makes the p-value at the end mean what you think it means, and its absence is exactly what the peeking problem exploits.

The peeking problem

Here is the most expensive mistake in everyday experimentation, and it feels like diligence. You launched a two-week test, and on day three the dashboard shows p < 0.05. The instinct — supported by every stakeholder who wants the win — is to call it and ship. That instinct is statistically catastrophic, and it has a name: peeking, or optional stopping.

The fixed-horizon math that produces a valid 5% false-positive rate assumes you look once, at the pre-committed sample size. Every additional look is another roll of the dice against the null, and the false-positive rate compounds with each one. Evan Miller's canonical essay states it without hedging: "Repeated significance testing always increases the rate of false positives, that is, you'll think many insignificant results are significant." The magnitude is not subtle — checking a test daily and stopping the instant it crosses the line can push the true false-positive rate from the nominal 5% toward 20–30%. You are not detecting wins faster; you are manufacturing false ones, because a test that wanders across p = 0.05 on its way to nowhere will eventually do so by chance if you give it enough chances.

The orthodox fix is a discipline of self-denial: decide on a sample size in advance and refuse to act on the running numbers until you reach it. That works and costs nothing technically, but it fights human nature and organizational pressure — nobody wants to ignore a "winning" dashboard for ten more days. Miller's essay points the way out by noting the assumption that creates the problem: the fixed-horizon test is fragile precisely because it permits exactly one look. "With Bayesian experiment design you can stop your experiment at any time and make perfectly valid inferences," he writes — a claim that motivated a decade of work on designs built to tolerate continuous monitoring, and that, as the frequentist-vs-Bayesian section shows, turned out to need serious qualification.

Sequential and always-valid inference

If your team will peek — and they will — the mature response is not to forbid it but to use a method whose math expects it. That is the premise of sequential testing, the cleanest answer to the peeking problem. As one platform's explainer puts it, "sequential testing is basically A/B testing that expects you to peek. Instead of pretending we won't peek, sequential testing acknowledges reality and adjusts the math accordingly." Rather than a single decision at a fixed sample size, sequential methods let you evaluate continuously while holding the overall false-positive rate at your chosen α.

The mechanism is a significance bar that moves with how often you look. "The magic happens through statistical adjustments that essentially raise the bar for significance as you check more often. Early in the test, you need overwhelming evidence to call a winner." Early on, when noise dominates, the bar is brutally high, so the occasional lucky swing can't trip a false win; as data accumulates and the estimate stabilizes, the bar relaxes toward the conventional level. This family — group-sequential designs, always-valid p-values, and confidence sequences (intervals that are valid at every moment, not just at the planned horizon) — is what modern experimentation platforms from Statsig, Eppo, and others have productized so that the stop-early button is safe to press.

The trade-off is real and worth stating plainly to stakeholders: the protection isn't free. To stay valid under continuous looking, sequential methods demand more total data to confirm a given effect than a perfectly-run fixed-horizon test would. You are buying the right to peek and stop early on big effects with a tax on detecting small ones. For most product organizations that trade is overwhelmingly worth it, because the realistic alternative was never a perfectly-disciplined fixed-horizon test — it was a fixed-horizon test corrupted by peeking, which is the worst of both worlds. Sequential testing's honesty about human behavior is its main virtue.

Frequentist vs. Bayesian — the live debate

This is the genuine schism in experimentation, and unlike many "controversies" it is not settled, not converging, and worth understanding rather than picking a tribe over. Practitioners disagree in good faith, and the right choice is contextual. The frequentist paradigm — null hypotheses, p-values, fixed sample sizes — is the default in most statistics education and most legacy tooling. The Bayesian paradigm reframes the question: instead of asking how surprising the data are under a no-effect assumption, it updates a prior belief into a posterior probability that the variant is better, given the data.

The everyday contrast is concrete. The frequentist approach "requires a fixed sample size, set in advance. Underpowered tests miss effects; overpowered tests detect trivial ones." The Bayesian approach instead "updates beliefs continuously as data comes in. Needs enough evidence for stable posteriors." The appeal of the Bayesian output is that it answers the question stakeholders actually ask — a direct probability the variant wins — rather than the contorted conditional a p-value reports. This is not a resolved question, and practitioners genuinely disagree on which to default to: one CRO expert reports that "when the MDEs are 5% or higher, I prefer to go Bayesian. It gives directional answers like 'there's a 92% chance the variant is better,' which helps teams act fast," while another goes "Frequentist almost exclusively for the simple reason of having transparent error control and being able to plan tests in advance."

The single most important myth to retire — and the reason both traps above read identically — is that going Bayesian makes the peeking problem disappear. It does not. A careful empirical treatment finds that "Bayesian posteriors remain interpretable under continuous monitoring, but a fixed posterior threshold does not control false positives when you peek and stop on success." Worse, the failure scales the same way: "the more you peek the higher the false positive rate." Which raises the deflating question the same author poses — "what advantage does it have over frequentist methods?" The honest synthesis, on which the careful voices converge, is that optional stopping inflates error under either paradigm unless the stopping rule is built into the math (which is what sequential and always-valid methods do, in both flavors). Choose your paradigm for its communication and workflow fit, not in the belief that one of them repeals the laws that govern looking at data repeatedly.

Variance reduction — buying power without buying traffic

Recall that power rises as variance falls. That single fact is the lever behind the most consequential efficiency technique in modern experimentation: variance reduction, and specifically CUPED. The acronym unpacks to Controlled-experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data, and the idea is to "reduce metric noise by leveraging historical user behavior." The mechanism is to "adjust each user's outcome by subtracting the predictable component estimated from pre-experiment behavior."

The intuition is worth carrying because it explains why this is close to a free lunch. A heavy spender will post a big number in both control and treatment; most of that user's outcome is predictable from what they did before the experiment and has nothing to do with your change. CUPED estimates that predictable baseline and subtracts it, leaving a residual that isolates the experiment-induced movement. The metric's expectation is unchanged — you are not biasing the result — but its variance drops, often substantially, which (per the power relationship) means a tighter confidence interval at the same sample size, or equivalently the same precision in materially less time. On high-variance metrics like revenue-per-user, where the noise from a few whales can swamp a real effect, the runtime savings are large enough to change which tests are feasible at all.

For a senior PM the strategic read is that variance reduction quietly expands the boundary of what is experimentally answerable. Tests that were "too slow to bother with" on a low-traffic surface or a noisy revenue metric can become tractable, which shifts more decisions from opinion to evidence. It is also why the sophistication of an experimentation platform is measured less by its dashboard than by whether it ships variance reduction, sequential testing, and trustworthy diagnostics by default — the statistics, not the UI, are where the leverage lives.

Trustworthy experiments and Twyman's law

The throughline of the Kohavi–Tang–Xu canon is in its title: the hard problem is not computing a p-value, it is trusting the number that goes into it. A statistically immaculate analysis of corrupted data is worse than useless, because it launders a bug into a confident decision. The reflex that defends against this is old enough to have a name. Twyman's law holds that "any figure that looks interesting or different is usually wrong," for the deep reason that "the more unusual or interesting the data, the more likely they are to have been the result of an error of one kind or another."

Operationally, Twyman's law inverts the celebration instinct. A result showing a 40% lift on a core metric is not a triumph to ship; it is an alarm to investigate. Such effects are far more often instrumentation bugs, logging double-counts, bot traffic, or a broken randomizer than genuine product magic. The discipline is to treat surprising wins as guilty until audited — to spend the suspicious result's credibility on a trust check before spending it on a roadmap decision.

The most important single trust check has a name and appears across the canon: sample ratio mismatch. As the summary of Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments puts it, when a test is designed for a balanced split, "deviating from the actual ratio of randomized unit in an experiment results in inaccurate results." If you assigned 50/50 but observe 52/48 at scale, something is broken upstream — a redirect dropping users, a bucketing bug, a bot filter applied unevenly — and the imbalance almost always correlates with the metric, biasing it. An SRM is not a small data-quality footnote; it is a hard invalidation. The mature programs run an SRM check automatically on every experiment and refuse to report results that fail it, because no amount of downstream statistical rigor can rescue a sample that wasn't randomized the way you think it was. Trust is the foundation; the math is the building on top.

Classic pitfalls that survive a clean p-value

Some of the most damaging experimentation errors pass every significance check, because they corrupt the comparison itself rather than the arithmetic. Two deserve permanent residence in a PM's mental checklist.

The first is Simpson's paradox: "a trend appears in several groups of data but disappears or reverses when the groups are combined." In experimentation it surfaces as a variant that looks better in aggregate but worse within every segment — or the reverse — and the usual culprit is an uneven mix between arms or across time. If your treatment skewed toward high-converting mobile users while control skewed desktop, the aggregate can tell a story that flips the moment you condition on platform. The defense is to pre-declare the unit and population of analysis and to verify that the segment-level and aggregate narratives agree before trusting either; when they diverge, the aggregate is usually the liar.

The second is the multiple comparisons problem, the statistical engine behind the metric-fishing this primer warned about at the OEC stage. The mechanics are unforgiving: "each test has its own chance of a Type I error (false positive), so the overall probability of making at least one false positive increases as the number of tests grows." Test twenty metrics at α = 0.05 on a change that truly does nothing and you should expect roughly one "significant" result purely from noise. The same logic indicts slicing one experiment across many segments hunting for a winner: "as more attributes are compared, it becomes increasingly likely that the treatment and control groups will appear to differ on at least one attribute due to random sampling error alone." The corrections — Bonferroni's blunt threshold-tightening, or the more powerful Benjamini–Hochberg false-discovery-rate control — restore honesty when you genuinely must test many hypotheses. But the cheaper and more robust defense is the one from the second section: pick one primary metric before launch, and treat everything else as a hypothesis-generator for the next experiment, not as a license to declare victory on whichever number happened to cross the line.

When not to experiment

A primer that only sold experimentation would be doing the opposite of what good experimenters do — and whether A/B testing should be the default decision tool is itself a genuine, ongoing debate among serious practitioners. The case for restraint is statistical before it is philosophical. The frequentist machinery is explicit that "underpowered tests miss effects" — and on a low-traffic surface, with a small baseline rate and a realistic MDE, the sample size required can exceed your entire user base or take a year to accumulate. Running the test anyway produces a flat result that means "we couldn't see," not "there's nothing there," and teams routinely misread the former as the latter. When power is structurally unavailable, an experiment launders ignorance into false confidence; a well-reasoned decision plus qualitative evidence is the more honest path.

The second limit is the gap between what's significant and what matters. Because "statistical significance is not the same as ... practical significance" and a "statistically significant result can be for an outcome so small in magnitude that it has no practical value," a test optimized to detect any movement will hand you wins too small to justify the maintenance burden of the code that produced them. Some decisions are also too consequential, too slow-burning, or too entangled to test cleanly: a brand repositioning, a pricing-model overhaul, a change whose real payoff is a long-term retention effect that a two-week window can't see, or a feature with strong network effects where splitting users violates the independence the math assumes. And one-way-door decisions with obvious upside sometimes shouldn't wait for evidence at all — the cost of the test exceeds the cost of being wrong.

The synthesis is not anti-experiment; it is that experimentation is one instrument in a portfolio that also holds qualitative research, strategic judgment, and reversible bets. The mark of a mature PM is matching the decision to the tool: experiment where you have the traffic, the runtime, and an effect size worth resolving — and have the discipline to decide by reasoning where you don't, rather than dressing a guess in a p-value.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

This primer points at neighbors rather than teaching them; each is a primer of its own.

  • Metrics & the metrics stack — the OEC and guardrails here are downstream of how you define north-star and input metrics; one hop to metric design and counter-metrics.
  • Behavioral psychology — novelty and primacy effects (users react to change before settling) are why experiment results drift over the first weeks; the bias catalog explains many of the lifts you'll measure.
  • Causal inference beyond A/B — when you can't randomize, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, instrumental variables, and switchback tests recover causal estimates under stated assumptions.
  • Experimentation platforms & culture — the organizational layer: feature flags, holdouts, an experiment review council, and the trust diagnostics (SRM checks, A/A tests) that make a high-volume program safe.
  • Data quality & instrumentation — Twyman's law sends you here; the reliability of event logging and identity resolution is upstream of every number in this primer.
  • Decision-making under uncertainty — expected value, one-way vs. two-way doors, and when a reversible bet beats waiting for significance.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-15_experimentation-and-statistics.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
